mappings.rationale: Directly covers the art of motion pictures, including the roles of director, camera, and technology in the creative process (A.1, A.2).
Traces cinema's historical development from the Lumiere brothers through sound, color, and the transition to digital (A, C.3).
Examines the Hollywood studio system -- from the golden age of the majors to the New Hollywood -- directly relevant to the motion-picture industry and classical film production (C.1, A.3).
Traces early, transitional, and classical silent film from Melies and Porter through Chaplin and Keaton, illuminating the evolution of film technology (A.2.e, C.3).
Analyzes film noir as a major motion-picture classification and artistic style, from its 1940s roots to neo-noir (A.1).
Examines documentary as a classification of motion pictures, tracing its development from Grierson's social realism to modern advocacy and propaganda uses (A.1, C.2).
Directly covers the interrelation of music with motion pictures, from silent film accompaniment to contemporary scoring (B).
Surveys a major non-Western cinema tradition -- from Phalke's mythologicals to Bollywood and parallel cinema -- relevant to global motion-picture art and industry (A.1, C.1).
Explores French cinema from the Lumiere origins through poetic realism to the New Wave auteurs like Godard and Truffaut, illuminating the director's role and film as art (A.2.c, C.3).
Surveys British cinema history, from Hitchcock's early work through Ealing comedies to contemporary filmmaking, as a significant tradition in motion-picture art (C.1, C.3).
Photography is the foundational technology of cinema; addresses the camera, exposure, and visual composition central to filmmaking (A.2.e).
Explains the physics of sound including acoustics and recording, relevant to sound technology in film production (A.2.e, A.3).
Bunuel, Deren, and other Surrealist filmmakers profoundly influenced avant-garde and experimental cinema (A.1, B).
Traces horror from Nosferatu through the slasher era as a major film genre, alongside its literary roots, relevant to the classification of motion pictures (A.1).
